title: Chapter Nineteen: Round Seven
content: Day 28.
The mask drops at dinner.

Not with a sound — with an absence. The twins sit across from me at the long table, and something that has been present in every meal since I arrived is simply gone. The performance. The curated sweetness, the synchronized smiles, the careful choreography of two children who are not children. Stripped away like wallpaper, and underneath: bare plaster. Something raw and assessing and utterly without pretense.

Flora eats her soup without lifting her eyes from the bowl. She spoons it methodically, a mechanical motion, and her face — that pale, heart-shaped face that has launched ten million views — is expressionless. Not blank. Empty. The way a room is empty after the furniture has been removed. The shape of what was there is still visible in the dust outlines on the floor, but the thing itself is gone.

Miles sets down his fork. The tines click against porcelain. He looks at me — not the way he's looked at me before, with the calculated warmth or the theatrical menace, both of which I've learned to categorize and file. This is something new. An appraising gaze, flat and professional, the way a surgeon looks at a body on the table. Not cruel. Clinical.
"Shall we begin?"

His tone is pleasant. Like asking about the weather. Like proposing a walk in the garden. The words hang in the air between us and I smell the rosemary from the lamb and the faint acid of the wine I haven't touched and something else, something metallic underneath, the smell of a house that has decided to stop pretending.

Flora's spoon pauses mid-motion. Her eyes flick to me — just once, quick as a bird's — and then back to her soup. But in that half-second I see it: recognition. She knows something has changed in me too. She doesn't know what. She's trying to read the shift, the way I'm reading hers. Two people at a table, each aware the other is holding cards, neither willing to show a hand.
"Round Seven," I say.
Miles smiles. Not the wide grin. Not the politician's warmth. Something smaller. Private. "Round Seven."

I pick up my fork. I cut a piece of lamb. I chew. The meat is tender, rosemary-sweet, and my jaw works mechanically and I taste nothing because my mouth has become an instrument of function, not sensation. I swallow.
"Rules?" I ask.

"New ones." Miles folds his napkin. Sets it beside his plate with the precision of someone arranging a flower. "The old Game required deception. You had to believe it was real. Now you know it's real. So the question becomes: what happens when the player sees the board?"
"She plays," I say.

"She plays." He nods, almost approving. "But Round Seven is different, Maeve. The first six rounds are about breaking you down. Round Seven is about what's left when you're broken. That's what Peter wanted to understand. The material beneath the stress."
The word *material* lands in my chest. That's what I am to him. Material. Substrate. The thing you test so you can observe the fracture pattern.
"And what's the method?" I ask.
Miles picks up his wine glass. The red liquid catches the candlelight and turns it into something arterial. "You'll see."
Flora's spoon scrapes the bottom of her bowl. The sound is thin and final, like a period at the end of a sentence.
Day 29.
Miles makes breakfast.

I come downstairs at seven — I haven't slept, but I've trained myself to keep the schedule, to move through the motions of routine so the house records normalcy — and the kitchen is not empty. Miles is at the counter. He's cracking eggs into a pan with one hand, the motion fluid and practiced, and the kitchen smells of butter and warm bread and something sharp and fresh that I identify, after a moment of bewilderment, as orange juice. Fresh-squeezed. He must have spent twenty minutes on it.
"Good morning," he says. The same tone as last night. Pleasant. Unremarkable. As if nothing happened. As if Round Seven is a weather system that has already moved on.

He sets a plate in front of me. Scrambled eggs — soft, exactly the way I make them when I'm alone, which means he's been watching me more closely than I realized. Toast cut diagonally. A glass of orange juice so bright it looks like a lamp. And beside the plate, in a small crystal vase I've never seen before: a single flower. A white rose, just opened, its petals touched with pink at the edges.

I stare at it. The flower is beautiful. The breakfast is beautiful. The boy who made it is beautiful, standing by the counter in his grey jumper, watching me with something in his eyes that might be warmth or might be the performance of warmth, and I can't tell the difference, and that's the point.
"Eat," he says. "You'll need your strength."

I eat. The eggs are perfect. The rose smells like nothing — or maybe my nose has stopped working, maybe Bly has finally desensitized me to the scent of beautiful things. I eat every bite. I drink the juice. I feel his gaze on me the whole time, cataloguing, measuring, running whatever calculation Round Seven requires.
Flora doesn't come down.
Day 30.
The house is silent.

I wake at six. The corridor is empty. The kitchen is cold — no stove, no pan, no orange juice, no rose. No Miles. I check the breakfast room. The table is bare. The chairs are pushed in. The candlesticks from last night's dinner are gone, and the sideboard has been wiped clean, and the room has the sterile, uninhabited look of a furniture showroom.
I search. East wing, west wing, the library, the conservatory, the garden. I check the rooms I know and the rooms I've only glimpsed. Nothing. No sound. No footstep. No voice.

Mrs. Grose is gone too — or at least, not visible. Her kitchen is immaculate and untouched, the same mug upturned by the drain, the same cold stove. As if the house has been wound down, placed on standby, every human element withdrawn to some location I can't access.

I stand in the main hall at noon and listen. The silence isn't just the absence of sound. It's a presence. A weight. The house is holding its breath. The portraits look down with their oil-painted eyes and the grandfather clock ticks in the hall — that one sound, that mechanical heartbeat, marking time in a place where time has stopped meaning anything.

I make myself a cup of tea. I drink it at the kitchen table. The cold seeps through my socks from the tile floor. The radiator clicks and produces nothing, a mouth opening and closing on words it can't say.
At twelve-seventeen, I hear a door close somewhere above me. Footsteps on the landing. Then nothing.
At twelve-forty-three, Flora appears in the kitchen doorway. She's wearing a different outfit from yesterday — a dark blue dress, her hair pulled back. She looks at me and says nothing.
"Where were you?" I ask.
"Here," she says.
"The house was empty."
"The house is never empty." She walks to the counter. Opens a drawer. Takes out an apple. Bites into it. The sound is sharp, wet, a small violence in the silence. "You just couldn't find us."
She leaves. Her footsteps recede up the stairs. The apple core sits on the counter, browning in the air.

I understand then. The absence was the round. They're teaching me that their presence is a privilege, not a guarantee. That warmth and cold come from the same hand. That I can be made alone in a house of three other people — that isolation isn't about distance, it's about access. They control whether I see them. They control whether the house feels populated or abandoned. They control the weather of this place.
I write it down. Day 30. Isolation protocol. Alternating warmth and absence. Objective: dependency. Method: unpredictability.
My handwriting is steady. That's something.
Day 31.
Flora catches me in the corridor.

I'm walking from the library to the stairwell — reading Peter's files on the laptop, cataloguing the incident logs, cross-referencing Jessel's timeline with the official documentation — and she's coming from the opposite direction. I move right to let her pass. Standard corridor etiquette. The kind of thoughtless accommodation you make for another body in a shared space.
She doesn't move right. She moves left. Into me.

Her hand brushes past my wrist and there's a bright, specific pain — a line of fire along the inside of my forearm. I gasp. Not dramatic, just involuntary, the sound a body makes when it's surprised by damage. I look down.

A thin red line. A scratch. Her fingernail — she was reaching for the wall, she'll say, or adjusting her sleeve, or any of the hundred small lies that explain why a fingernail would catch skin and drag. The scratch is shallow but it bleeds. A thin bead of red, dark as wine, rolling slowly down my forearm toward my wrist.
"Sorry." Flora's voice is flat. Not concerned. Not amused. Just present. "I'm so clumsy."

She looks at me. The scratch is between us, visible, undeniable. A small violence, the kind that doesn't leave a scar, the kind you'd never report, the kind that exists in the space between accident and intention where plausible deniability breeds. Her eyes meet mine and there's something in them — not satisfaction, not cruelty. Something closer to compulsion. As if her hand moved on instructions she didn't write.

She walks away. I stand in the corridor with the blood running down my arm and I smell it — copper, thin and clean and unmistakable. The smell of my own body opened. A small opening. The smallest. But an opening nonetheless.

I go to my room. I clean the scratch with antiseptic. I photograph it. Timestamp: Day 31, 14:22. Location: east corridor, second floor. Perpetrator: Flora Ashford. Method: fingernail. Cover story: clumsiness.
I add it to the file.
Day 32.
Miles closes the door on my hand.

I'm leaving the breakfast room — he ate with me this morning, toast and silence, no flower, no warmth, the pendulum swung back to cold — and I reach for the door handle at the same moment he pulls it from the other side. The door swings. My left hand is on the jamb. The door catches my fingers between its edge and the frame.

The pain is immediate and total. Not sharp like the scratch — deep. A grinding compression that radiates from my knuckles up through my wrist and into my forearm. I make a sound. I can't help it. A low, animal noise, the kind that comes from a place beneath language.

The door opens. Miles stands on the other side. He looks at my hand — cradled against my chest, the knuckles already darkening, the bruise blooming under the skin like something growing in time-lapse. His expression is concern. Perfect concern. The right amount of worry, the right angle of the eyebrows, the slight parting of the lips that signals distress.
"These old houses," he says. "The doors are so heavy."

He reaches out. He takes my injured hand in his. His fingers are cool and dry and he holds my hand the way a doctor might — gently, professionally, with the detachment of someone assessing damage rather than comforting the damaged. He turns my hand over, examining the bruise. His thumb traces the edge of the discoloration, pressing lightly — not enough to hurt more, but enough to remind me that he could. That the pressure is his to apply or withhold.
"You should put ice on that," he says. He releases my hand. "We wouldn't want it to swell."
He walks away. His footsteps are unhurried. His posture is relaxed. A boy concerned about his governess's injured hand. A model of solicitude.

I stand in the doorway with my hand throbbing and my vision narrowed to a single point of focus — the door frame, the edge of it, the exact angle that caught my fingers. I could have moved. I could have pulled back. I'm quick. I've been quick since Round One. But he was quicker. He knew exactly when I would reach, exactly where my hand would be. This wasn't an accident.

The bruise will be purple by tonight. Yellow by the end of the week. A small wound. The kind that heals. The kind that doesn't leave a scar. The kind you'd have to document, because no one would believe it mattered.
I photograph my hand. I timestamp it. I add it to the file.
The file is becoming the most comprehensive abuse dossier in the history of Bly Manor.

I know this because Peter's files — the ones on the USB, the ones Mrs. Grose copied before the locks changed — they're thorough. Clinical. Forty-seven pages of design document. Subfolders of incident logs and psychological profiles. But they document the *system*. The architecture. They don't document what it feels like to have your hand closed in a door by a child who smiles while he does it.
My file does.

Phone footage. I've been recording — short clips, ten seconds each, the phone held low and unobtrusive. Flora's face when she scratched me. Miles's "concern" at the door. Timestamps on everything. Locations. I screenshot the security feed I've accessed through the laptop — Peter's old credentials still work, or maybe the system hasn't been updated since he died, or maybe no one imagined the governess would find her way into the control room. I have footage of corridors I've never walked through. Rooms I've never seen. Blind spots the twins don't know exist.

I'm building a case. Not for the police. Not yet. The police require a threshold of evidence I haven't crossed — a bruise, a scratch, a pattern of behavior that could be explained away by old houses and clumsy children and the unreliability of a woman who was institutionalized at seventeen. The internet doesn't require a threshold. The internet requires a story.

But I don't fight back. Not yet. I smile at breakfast. I say "thank you" when Miles shows concern. I clean my scratch with antiseptic and I ice my hand and I say nothing. Let them think the small violences are working. Let them think I'm absorbing, the way Flora described — *when she stopped fighting*. Let them believe the Game is progressing according to design.
I need more. I need the audience.
The control room is cold.

Not the distant, ambient cold of the rest of Bly — this is a specific cold, the cold of electronics running constantly, the cold of servers and processors and hard drives spinning in a room without windows. My breath doesn't fog, but it might as well. The air is sharp and metallic and sterile, stripped of everything organic, and my skin prickles the moment I step through the door.

I used the red fob key. The copy I made from Mrs. Grose's keyring three days ago, pressed into a bar of soap while she was in the garden, then taken to the village on the pretext of buying stamps. A hardware store. A key-cutting machine. Two pounds and a nod from a man who didn't ask questions. The original back on the ring before Mrs. Grose returned. She knows, probably. She knows everything. She hasn't said a word.
The fob works. The light flashes green. The door opens.

The monitors fill the far wall. Nine screens, arranged in a grid, each one showing a different feed. The kitchen. The breakfast room. The main hall. The east corridor. The west corridor. The garden. The lake. The stairwell. And one I don't recognize — a dark room, stone walls, no furniture. A cellar, maybe. Or a room that doesn't appear on any floor plan.

I sit at the console. The chair is the same one I sat in when I first accessed Peter's files, when I read the design document and watched Jessel walk into the lake and vomited into the wastebasket. The wastebasket is still there. It's been emptied. Someone knew I'd been here. Someone cleaned up after me. Someone is always watching, even when there's no one in the room.
I pull up the livestream settings.

The system is professional. Peter's design, or an upgrade since his death — multi-camera ingest, cloud backup, remote access. The twins have been streaming their TikTok content from somewhere, and this is where. The interface is clean, commercial-grade, the kind of software a production company would use. Bly Manor isn't just a house. It's a studio. Every corridor is a set. Every room is a stage. And I've been on camera since the day I arrived.

I navigate to the broadcast module. Set up a new stream. Private link. No public listing. No searchable title. Just a URL — long, random, unguessable — and a password I generate from the alphanumeric string on the back of the USB drive. Caroline's name, encoded. My mother, protecting me from beyond the grave, or at least from beyond the files.
I test the feed.

The quality is perfect. Every corridor, every room, every blind spot — all nine cameras, streaming simultaneously, the resolution crystalline, the audio crisp enough to hear the clock ticking in the main hall three stories below. I can see the kitchen. The empty stove. The mug by the drain. I can see the garden, the lake grey and still, the lawn sweeping down to the water's edge. I can see the east corridor — the spot where Flora scratched me, the wall I pressed against when the blood ran down my arm.

I open the stream on my phone. The feed loads. I'm watching myself on the screen — or rather, I'm watching the room I just left, the control room desk, the back of my own head on one of the nine monitors, a recursive image, a camera watching a camera watching a camera.
Bly Manor on camera. All of it. Every angle. Every moment.
I close the test feed. I save the settings. I log out.

My hands are shaking. The cold of the room is in my bones now, the specific cold of electronics and ambition, and I flex my bruised fingers and feel the throb answer back — a reminder, a small persistent voice saying *this is real, this is happening, every bruise and every scratch and every silence is real and documented and waiting*.

I sit on my bed with the laptop balanced on my knees. The screen casts blue light across my face, across the walls, across the ceiling. My room is dark except for the screen. The radiator clicks. The house is quiet — the particular quiet of a place that knows something is about to change.

I open Reddit. The old account. The one that went viral, that attracted the true-crime obsessives and the armchair psychologists and the people who called me a victim and the people who called me a villain and the one person — @BlyWatcher — who said *she has every reason to be angry*.
I click "Create Post."
My fingers move. The words come.
**Title:** I'm going to livestream from inside a house that's trying to kill me. Tune in at 8 PM GMT.

**Body:** I'm the Bly Manor nanny. Yes, that one. The one you decided was crazy, or faking, or both. I've been documenting everything for five weeks. Video. Audio. Timestamps. I have access to the security cameras. I have the original design documents for the "Game" they've been playing. I have footage of the children's "accidents." I'm going to show you all of it. Live. Unedited. You can decide for yourselves what's happening here. Link will be posted at 8 PM GMT. Be there.
I read it back. The words are clean. Direct. The kind of post that makes people click.
I save it as a draft.
I don't post it. Not yet.

I've been here before. Twice. The first time, I wrote the post and deleted it, because the internet had turned on me and I couldn't bear to give it more ammunition. The second time, I wrote it and closed the tab, because I wasn't ready to give up control of the narrative. Both times, my finger hovered over the orange button. Both times, I pulled back.

This time is different. This time I have the cameras. This time I have the files. This time I have the proof — not just Jessel's journal and Peter's design document and the USB drive, but my own body, marked in small ways by small violences that add up to a pattern no reasonable person could dismiss as accident.

The audience is the card I've been holding since I arrived. The most powerful card in the game. If I play it, the twins lose their invisibility. The house loses its privacy. Every corridor becomes a courtroom. Every room becomes evidence. The Game only works in darkness — Peter wrote that himself, in the design document, in a footnote I almost missed: *The system requires absence of external observation to function as designed.* Remove the darkness and the system fails.
But.

The internet is not my friend. It's not anyone's friend. It's a weapon, and weapons cut both ways. The same audience that watches the livestream will interpret what it sees through a hundred thousand lenses — some sympathetic, some hostile, some indifferent, some predatory. They'll zoom in on my face and analyze my expressions. They'll dissect my tone of voice. They'll find my hospital records again, my thesis, my history, and they'll use it to build a narrative I can't control. I know this. I've watched them do it.

Once I press Post, the Game becomes public. No takebacks. No privacy. No safety net. The internet will be inside Bly Manor with me, and it won't leave when I want it to, and it will have opinions about everything it sees, and those opinions will become part of the story whether I want them to or not.
I stare at the draft. The cursor blinks. The blue light of the screen fills the room like water.
My finger hovers over the Post button.
Third time.
This time, I'll actually press it.
